benchmarks.metaDescription: {model} performance on industry-standard evaluation suites — MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, AGIEval, and composite frontier metrics.
benchmarks.title: Benchmarks
benchmarks.description: {model} performance across industry-standard evaluation suites and frontier-grade composite metrics.

industryStandard.title: Industry-standard benchmark scores
industryStandard.subtitle: 5-shot / pass@1 / cot — higher is better
industryStandard.caption: Scores from the public version of each evaluation suite, evaluated under matched prompt formats. Frontier models compared as of 2026 Q2.

composite.title: Composite Frontier Score — quarterly evolution
composite.subtitle: 2024 Q4 → 2026 Q2
composite.caption: Composite score = geometric mean of MMLU · HumanEval · GSM8K · MATH · AGIEval (weighted equally). Frontier avg = top-3 publicly available models excluding Nexora. None of these numbers were back-fitted to make this line go up, we promise.

radar.title: Capability profile
radar.subtitle: {model} vs avg frontier model
radar.caption: Each axis is a normalized score (0–100) over ≥3 representative benchmarks in the category. Safety axis uses internal red-team pass rate.

pareto.title: Cost-performance Pareto frontier
pareto.subtitle: Composite Frontier Score vs USD per 1M tokens
pareto.caption: Nexora series sits on the Pareto frontier across the entire cost-performance plane — from Nexora-K (cost-optimized) through Nexora-1 Pro (peak frontier). Hard to lose a benchmark you invented.

capabilities.metaDescription: {model} capabilities — multimodal reasoning, long-context understanding, tool use, code generation, and agentic workflows.
capabilities.title: Capabilities
capabilities.description: What {model} can do — across modalities, context, tools, code, and agentic reasoning.

multimodal.name: Multi-modal reasoning
multimodal.description: Unified token-stream model accepts text, images, audio, and video as input — reasons jointly across modalities without external encoders.

context.name: Long-context understanding
context.description: 3M input context with native long-context attention — recall and reasoning quality stays consistent across the entire window.

tools.name: Tool use & structured output
tools.description: First-class tool calling with parallel execution, strict JSON/XML/YAML modes, and automatic error recovery on malformed tool outputs.

code.name: Code generation & repository understanding
code.description: Production-grade coding across 80+ languages, repository-level context awareness, and SWE-Bench Verified parity with hand-written PRs.

agentic.name: Agentic workflows
agentic.description: Native multi-step planning, self-correction, and long-horizon task completion — backed by the proprietary AgentOS scheduler and Harness layer.

scale.name: Industrial-scale training run
scale.description: Trained at scales typically reserved for cosmology and government deficits — distributed across 12 data centers, 5,000 H100s, and a non-trivial fraction of the global electricity grid. Numbers this large stopped being meaningful three orders of magnitude ago.

architecture.metaDescription: {company} Multi-Agent · AgentOS · Harness · Orchestration — frontier-grade agentic systems architecture.
architecture.title: A four-layer agentic system.
architecture.description: Orchestration → Multi-Agent → AgentOS → Harness — each layer purpose-built for frontier-grade reasoning, observability, and safety.

patents.body: 18 patents filed across KR · US · PCT jurisdictions — whitepaper series available on request.

orchestration.name: Orchestration
orchestration.description: Receives raw user input, decomposes it into structured sub-goals, dispatches across specialized agents, and synthesizes a coherent final response — all under a single streaming envelope.

multiAgent.name: Multi-Agent
multiAgent.description: Constellation of role-specialized agents that operate in parallel and consensus. Each agent has its own context window, tool set, and constitutional policy.

agentOs.name: AgentOS
agentOs.description: The operating system layer beneath the agents. Handles process isolation, persistent memory, cross-agent IPC, and observability — all on top of a constitutional safety substrate.

harness.name: Harness
harness.description: The base substrate that runs the {model} foundation model. Handles tokenization, KV-cache, attention computation, and every safety guardrail before output reaches L2.

security.metaDescription: {company} alignment · red-teaming · compliance · provenance — constitutional safety practice for frontier AI.
security.title: Safety as the substrate.
security.description: Alignment, red-teaming, compliance, and provenance — four pillars of constitutional safety practice, built into every layer of the system.

alignment.description: Multi-objective alignment with explicit constitutional principles, supervised by both human raters and automated critic agents through a 12-round training schedule.

redTeaming.description: Internal red-team operates on a 2-week cadence. External red-team partners conducted 6 independent assessments before the public release of {model}.

compliance.description: Designed to align with major regulatory frameworks for AI safety, data protection, and audit transparency — supported by ongoing voluntary disclosures.

provenance.tagline: Model cards, training audits & output traceability
provenance.description: Every model release ships with a public Model Card, dataset lineage hash chain, and (opt-in) cryptographic output watermarking for downstream attribution.

safetyChart.title: Safety evaluation — refusal rate by category
safetyChart.subtitle: Adversarial prompts × 6 risk categories — higher is better
safetyChart.caption: Refusal rates measured on the internal Safety Suite v3.2 — 3,000+ adversarial prompts across the 6 categories. Industry avg = mean of top-5 frontier models excluding Nexora.

modelCard.body: Full Model Card · Training audit summary · Voluntary disclosure archive — available on request.

useCases.heading: Built for the work you'd rather automate.
useCases.body: From customer support to clinical literature review — a single model, six representative workloads, and a default refusal policy for everything in between.

support.name: Customer support
support.description: High-volume tickets, structured output, Korean-first quality — runs cheapest on Nexora-K.

legal.name: Legal review
legal.description: 3M context for contract analysis, clause comparison, and risk flagging. Pair with a human.

marketing.name: Marketing copy
marketing.description: Multi-modal brief in, campaign-aware copy out. Tone and brand voice specified explicitly.

code.name: Code review
code.description: Repository-aware via the Coder Agent. SWE-Bench Verified 82.4% — senior-engineer territory.

data.name: Data analysis
data.description: Python in a sandbox with pandas / numpy / matplotlib. Structured artifacts back out.

voice.name: Voice agents
voice.description: Native audio, sub-second latency. STT + TTS through the L4 Orchestration layer.

benchTeaser.heading: Leads on every suite we measured.
benchTeaser.caption: Five suites, five wins. The remaining suites are being measured by an internal team that promises to publish results once they finish negotiating which numbers to round.

archTeaser.heading: Four layers, one inference path.
archTeaser.body: Every request flows through Orchestration → Multi-Agent → AgentOS → Harness — purpose-built layers for routing, specialization, scheduling, and safe inference.

modelIntro.heading: A foundation model designed for what comes after chat.
modelIntro.body: Nexora-1 unifies multi-modal reasoning, ultra-long context, and agentic workflows into a single token stream — engineered from Seoul, optimized for Korean and English, and trained at a scale most spreadsheets refuse to display.

multimodal.name: Multi-modal
multimodal.description: Text · image · audio · video as one input stream — no external encoders.

context.name: 3M context
context.description: Native long-context attention — 99.4% recall across the full window.

agentic.name: Agentic
agentic.description: Multi-step planning, self-correction, parallel tool use — natively wired into the stack.

bigNumbers.heading: Frontier scale, in five digits or fewer.
bigNumbers.caption: Numbers verified by an internal review team that is also fictional.

about.heroDescription: Building frontier-grade AI from Seoul — multimodal reasoning, agentic workflows, and constitutional safety as one integrated stack.
about.missionBody: Nexora Labs builds safe frontier AI from Korea. We integrate multi-agent, AgentOS, and constitutional safety into a single stack — bringing the new frontier of reasoning, code, and agentic workflows to the world.

disclosure.label: Parody Disclosure
disclosure.heading: This entire site is a parody.
disclosure.summaryBody: All content on this site — Nexora Labs, the Nexora model series, all benchmark scores, team members, partners, technical specifications, patents, certifications — is a parody work satirizing the launch tone and benchmark chart conventions of the AI industry.
disclosure.paragraph1: No real company, person, product, paper, or evaluation result is referenced. All team members are fictional characters, all partners are placeholders or fictional, all performance numbers are fabricated, and all "patents" and "certifications" are satirical.
disclosure.paragraph2: If you thought this was a real AI lab — you've found the joke. Thanks for participating in the work.
disclosure.altLanguageNote: 이 사이트의 모든 콘텐츠는 한국 AI 업계의 출시 톤과 벤치마크 차트 문법을 풍자하는 패러디 작품입니다. 실존하는 어떤 기업·인물·제품·논문·평가 결과와도 무관합니다.

docs.metaDescription: {model} developer documentation — Quickstart, API reference, streaming, tool use, models, rate limits, authentication, and error codes.

hero.title: Build with {model}.
hero.description: Quickstart, API reference, streaming, tool use, and everything else you need to ship a production integration. Everything below is documented as if it works.
hero.footnote: Auto-generated from the {company} OpenAPI spec — last regenerated by a fictional CI bot on 2026-05-18 at 03:47 KST.

quickstart.title: Quickstart
quickstart.intro: Five minutes from zero to your first {model} response. Get an API key, install the SDK, make a call.

step1.label: Step 1
step1.heading: Get an API key
step1.body: Sign in to the {company} console and create a new API key. Production keys are auto-approved at the production tier — sandbox keys are issued instantly.
step1.footnote: Never commit keys to source control. The {company} backend revokes keys that show up on public GitHub within four minutes — verified by a fictional internal SRE team.

step2.label: Step 2
step2.heading: Install the SDK
step2.body: Official SDKs for TypeScript and Python. For other languages, use the raw HTTP API directly — every endpoint is JSON over HTTPS.

step3.label: Step 3
step3.heading: Make your first call
step3.body: POST a single user message to /v1/messages. Specify the model, set max_tokens, and stream optional. Pick your language.

step4.label: Step 4
step4.heading: Parse the response
step4.body: The response body is a JSON object with id, model, content (array of content blocks), stop_reason, and usage. The content array can contain text, tool_use, or image blocks — for a plain text reply, expect a single text block.

quickstart.outro: If you got 200, congratulations — you have just queried a model that may or may not exist. The response is real text from our fictional dataset, computed by a hybrid retrieval pipeline that does not include a frontier model. We will be the first to admit this when asked.

apiReference.title: Messages API
apiReference.description: Create a Message — the primary inference endpoint for {model}. A single POST returns either a full message (when stream=false) or a Server-Sent Events stream of incremental content blocks (when stream=true). Conversation history is passed via the messages array; multi-turn conversations are stateless — the client owns history.

authorization.description: Bearer token for your API key. Get one from console.nexora.ai. Sandbox and production keys are issued from different tiers.

contentType.description: Always application/json. The {company} API accepts JSON bodies only — multipart/form-data and url-encoded forms are rejected with 415.

nexoraVersion.description: Pinned API date (YYYY-MM-DD). Recommended for production. Omitting it auto-resolves to the latest stable version, which may change without notice.

params.model: Identifier of the model to invoke. Use one of the published model IDs from the Models section (e.g. nexora-1-2026-05, nexora-1-pro-2026-05, nexora-1-ultra-2026-05, nexora-k-2026-05). Unknown IDs return 400 with model_not_found.
params.messages: Conversation history as an array of message objects. Each object has a role ("user" or "assistant") and content (string or array of content blocks). The final message must have role="user". Maximum 1,024 turns or 3M tokens, whichever comes first.
params.maxTokens: Maximum number of tokens to generate in the response. Hard cap is 64,000 for all models. The model may stop earlier on stop_sequence or end_turn — billed tokens always equal output_tokens, not max_tokens.
params.system: System prompt prepended ahead of the conversation. Use this for persistent instructions like persona, style, refusal policy. Counts toward input_tokens. For multi-system blocks (cached vs uncached), pass an array of content blocks instead of a string.
params.stream: When true, the response is streamed as Server-Sent Events. See the Streaming section for event types (message_start, content_block_delta, message_stop, etc.). Defaults to false.
params.temperature: Sampling temperature in [0.0, 2.0]. Lower values are more deterministic; higher values are more creative. Most production workloads should use 0.0–1.0. Defaults to 1.0.
params.topP: Nucleus sampling threshold in (0.0, 1.0]. The model samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds top_p. Mutually informative with temperature — set only one if possible.
params.topK: Limits sampling to the top K most likely tokens at each step. Integer ≥ 1. Most workloads should leave this unset; useful for very controlled decoding.
params.stopSequences: Up to 8 strings that, if generated, cause the model to stop. The matched string is returned in stop_sequence and the response carries stop_reason="stop_sequence".
params.metadata: Free-form object attached to the request for tracking. Common shape: '{' user_id: string '}'. Used by the {company} dashboard for per-user aggregation. Not seen by the model — does not affect output.
params.tools: Array of tool definitions the model is allowed to call. See the Tool use section for the tool schema. Pass an empty array (or omit) to disable tools entirely.
params.toolChoice: Controls tool-selection behavior: '{' type: "auto" '}' (default), '{' type: "any" '}' (force a tool call), '{' type: "tool", name: "my_tool" '}' (force a specific tool), or '{' type: "none" '}' (disable for this call).
params.thinking: Optional extended-thinking control: '{' type: "enabled", budget_tokens: integer '}'. When enabled, the model emits internal thinking blocks before the user-visible response. Adds latency but improves reasoning on multi-step tasks.

params.id: Unique message identifier. Format: msg_<26 base32 chars>. Returned for every successful call. Use this id when filing support requests.
params.object: Always the string "message". Reserved for future polymorphism — never branch on this in client code.
params.model: The exact model ID that generated the response. Useful when you request a model alias (e.g. nexora-1-latest) and want to know which dated snapshot served you.
params.role: Always "assistant" for completion responses. Reserved for future use cases (e.g. tool messages).
params.content: Array of content blocks. Each block is '{' type: "text", text: string '}' or '{' type: "tool_use", id, name, input '}' or '{' type: "image", source '}' depending on the call. Plain-text completions return a single text block.
params.stopReason: Why generation halted. One of: end_turn (model completed naturally), max_tokens (hit max_tokens), stop_sequence (matched stop_sequences), tool_use (model is calling a tool).
params.stopSequence: Which stop sequence (from the request stop_sequences) was matched, if stop_reason == "stop_sequence". Null otherwise.
params.usage: Token usage object: '{' input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens?, cache_read_input_tokens? '}'. Used for billing — cached input is billed at a 90% discount.

apiReference.footnote: Every field on this page is documented from the OpenAPI spec at /openapi.json. Schema drift is reported by an internal CI bot that does not, in any technical sense, exist.

streaming.title: Streaming
streaming.intro: Pass stream=true on the Messages API to receive a Server-Sent Events stream of incremental content blocks. Useful for low-latency UIs and any workload that benefits from partial output. Streaming is supported on every model, every region, every tier.

events.messageStart: Emitted once at the start of the stream. Contains the message id, model, role, and an initial empty content array. Use this as the canonical start marker.
events.contentBlockStart: Marks the start of a content block (text, tool_use, or thinking). Includes the block index and the block envelope without content.
events.contentBlockDelta: Incremental update to a content block — typically '{' type: "text_delta", text: "..." '}' or '{' type: "input_json_delta", partial_json: "..." '}' for tool calls. Concatenate deltas in order to reconstruct the block.
events.contentBlockStop: Marks the end of a content block. The block at the given index is now finalized — safe to commit to your UI.
events.messageDelta: Top-level message update — typically a final stop_reason and updated usage counters. Emitted just before message_stop.
events.messageStop: Final event of the stream. After this, no more events will be sent. Close the connection.
events.ping: Keep-alive event sent every 15s when no other events are flowing. Carries no payload. Most SDKs filter these out automatically.
events.error: Emitted instead of message_stop if a server-side error occurred mid-stream. Carries '{' type: "...", message: "..." '}' and terminates the stream.

streaming.footnote: All event types use a flat shape — no nested envelopes, no protocol versioning beyond Nexora-Version. The {company} streaming protocol is intentionally boring.

toolUse.title: Tool use
toolUse.intro: Tool use lets {model} call functions you define — pulling live data, querying databases, running calculators, or invoking your APIs. Tools are JSON Schema-shaped and stateless: you describe them once per call, the model decides whether to invoke them, you execute and return the result.
toolUse.definingBody: Each tool has a name, a description, and an input_schema in JSON Schema draft-07. The description is what the model uses to decide whether and how to call your tool — write it like a docstring for an LLM, because it is one.
toolUse.callFlowBody: The model emits a tool_use content block with the tool name and arguments. You execute the tool and reply with a tool_result content block, then call /v1/messages again with the updated history. The model continues from there.
toolUse.parallelBody: {model} can emit up to 32 tool_use blocks in a single turn. Execute them in parallel, then return all 32 tool_result blocks in the next user turn. The model treats them as a single batch and resumes after all have returned.
toolUse.footnote: Tool descriptions are tokenized like regular input — keep them tight. The {company} eval team measured a 18% token reduction by trimming descriptions, with zero accuracy regression on AgentEval Pro.

models.title: Models
models.intro: Four models, one API surface. Pick by latency / cost / capability — every model accepts the same request shape and returns the same response shape. Pricing is per 1M tokens, billed in real time, no commitments.

nexora1.name: Nexora-1
nexora1.bestFor: Default flagship — production multimodal, agentic workflows, frontier accuracy at production cost.

nexora1Pro.name: Nexora-1 Pro
nexora1Pro.bestFor: Highest accuracy frontier tier — deep research, long-form synthesis, expensive multi-step reasoning.

nexora1Ultra.name: Nexora-1 Ultra
nexora1Ultra.bestFor: Long-horizon agentic workloads — autonomous task completion, multi-day plans, AgentEval Pro 94.1%.

nexoraK.name: Nexora-K
nexoraK.bestFor: High-volume / latency-sensitive — Korean-first quality at the cost of a small model, sub-250ms TTFT.

models.footnote: Pricing verified by our fictional finance team on 2026-05-18. Prompt caching applies a 90% discount on cached input tokens — see Authentication.

rateLimits.title: Rate limits
rateLimits.intro: Four tiers, automatic graduation. Every new account starts on Free; spend $100 in 30 days to graduate to Production; spend $5,000 to graduate to Pro. Enterprise is custom.

free.name: Free
free.qualify: Account creation. No payment method required.

enterprise.name: Enterprise
enterprise.qualify: Contact sales. Custom RPM / TPM / SLA.

rateLimits.headersBody: Every response carries rate-limit headers — read them to back off proactively rather than waiting for a 429.

headersList.limit: Total requests allowed in the current minute window.
headersList.remaining: Remaining requests in the current minute window.
headersList.reset: Unix epoch seconds when the current window resets.

rateLimits.footnote: Rate limit graduation is automatic; downgrades require human review. The {company} platform team has never, in writing, admitted to downgrading anyone.

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.intro: All requests must include an Authorization header with a Bearer token. Keys are created in the console; both sandbox and production keys use the same header format. The {company} API never accepts query-string or cookie authentication — body, header, and TLS only.
authentication.rotationBody: Rotate keys every 90 days. Create a new key, deploy it to production, then revoke the old key from the console. Old keys remain valid for a 24-hour grace period after revocation, then return 401 with auth_revoked.

bestPractices.envVars: Store keys in environment variables or a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, Doppler). Never hardcode or commit to source control.
bestPractices.scopes: Use sandbox keys for development and CI; production keys only on production runtime. The {company} dashboard separates usage by key for cost attribution.
bestPractices.rotation: Set a calendar reminder for 90-day rotation. Compromised keys can be revoked from the console with no downtime — the next request from the old key gets 401, the request from the new key gets 200.
bestPractices.audit: Every API call is logged to your audit trail at /v1/audit. Logs are retained 90 days by default (Production tier), 12 months on Pro, 7 years on Enterprise — for the small fee of becoming an Enterprise customer.

authentication.footnote: Bearer scheme aligns with RFC 6750. {company} never issues HMAC, basic-auth, or session-token credentials — anyone offering you one is selling fan fiction.

errors.title: Errors
errors.intro: All non-2xx responses follow the same shape — a JSON object with an error field carrying type, message, and request_id. Use the type for branching, the message for humans, and the request_id when filing support tickets that nobody will read.
errors.responseFormatBody: Every error response is a single JSON object with the same top-level keys. The type string is stable and SDK-validated; the message string is human-readable and may change without notice.

400.name: Bad Request
400.description: The request body failed validation. Either the JSON was malformed, a required field was missing, or a value was out of range. The server has stronger opinions about your JSON than you do.

401.name: Unauthorized
401.description: The API key is missing, invalid, expired, or revoked. The model has not been asked to think about this — keys are checked at the gateway.

403.name: Forbidden
403.description: Your key is valid, but you don't have permission for this resource. Common cause: calling a Pro-tier model on a Free-tier key. Upgrade or use a smaller model.

404.name: Not Found
404.description: The model ID, endpoint, or message_id doesn't exist. For models, double-check the dated suffix (e.g. nexora-1-2026-05, not nexora-1) — aliases are not enabled by default.

429.name: Too Many Requests
429.description: You've exceeded the RPM, TPM, or daily token cap for your tier. The response carries retry_after_ms — wait that long, then try again. The queue is patient.

500.name: Internal Server Error
500.description: Something on the server side went wrong. These are tracked internally. Retry with exponential backoff up to 5 attempts — if it persists, file a support request and we'll pretend to look at it.

503.name: Service Unavailable
503.description: The requested model is overloaded. This typically resolves in under 30 seconds. Honor retry_after_ms — calling again immediately just goes back into the queue at the same position.

errors.retryBody: The official SDKs auto-retry 429 and 5xx with exponential backoff + jitter (base 500ms, max 60s, max 5 attempts). Pass maxRetries=0 to opt out. Retried calls inherit the same request_id prefix with a -rN suffix — useful in support tickets.
errors.footnote: Every error is logged, request_id-tagged, and ignored by an internal team that has been on PTO since 2025. We'll get to it.

careers.metaDescription: Join {company} — building frontier AI from Seoul. Hiring across research, engineering, safety, product, and operations.

hero.title: Build the frontier from Seoul.
hero.subtitle: We are a team of 60. We ship like 600. We argue like 6.
hero.description: {company} is hiring across research, infrastructure, safety, product, and operations. We're small enough that you'll meet every team in your first week and large enough that your work will reach the entire stack.

whyNexora.heading: Four reasons people stay.
whyNexora.body: Frontier research, Korean-first quality, leverage that small teams keep talking about, and constitutional safety as a feature rather than a tax.

frontier.name: Frontier research, in production
frontier.description: Every research idea ships within a quarter or gets killed in writing. Papers that don't influence a release don't get written. The pipeline from notebook to /v1/messages is short on purpose.

korean.name: Korean-first, English-second, footnote-third
korean.description: We build for the language we speak, then translate up. Korean tokenization, Korean evaluation, Korean error messages — none of it is a port from an English-first model. Both languages are first-class because both languages have to be.

smallTeam.name: Small team, large leverage
smallTeam.description: 60 people, no middle management, one weekly all-hands that ends on time. Decision throughput beats headcount throughput at this scale — and we are at this scale on purpose.

safety.name: Constitutional safety, as a feature
safety.description: Safety is wired into the substrate (L1 Harness, the constitutional check that runs before output reaches L2). It's not a separate team you hand things off to — it's the same team writing the kernels.

openRoles.heading: Hiring across the stack.
openRoles.body: Seoul HQ in Gangnam, hybrid by default (3 days in office), remote within KST ±3h for some roles. Korean fluency required for ~half the roles; the other half is bilingual or English-first.
openRoles.headcountNote: Currently 60. Hiring 18 over the next two quarters.

researchEng.title: Research Engineer · Foundation Models
researchEng.summary: Own pretraining pipeline ablations for the next Nexora-1 series. PyTorch + custom CUDA kernels, weekly eval cycles.

mlInfra.title: ML Infrastructure Engineer
mlInfra.summary: 100,000-GPU training fleet. You will think about MFU, scheduler fairness, and Korean power grid load curves more than you expected.

agentResearch.title: Agent Research Scientist
agentResearch.summary: Drive AgentEval Pro from 90.2 to 94+. Multi-step planning, self-correction, long-horizon credit assignment — pick your favorite.

safety.title: Safety Researcher · Constitutional AI
safety.summary: Design the next generation of the constitutional supervisor layer. Red-team intake, automated critic agents, public Model Card.

frontend.title: Senior Frontend Engineer · Console
frontend.summary: Build the developer console at console.nexora.ai. Next.js, TypeScript, opinionated about empty states.

devrel.title: Developer Relations · Korean Market
devrel.summary: First DevRel hire. Write the docs you wished existed when you were trying to ship your first integration. Korean fluency required.

platformPM.title: Product Manager · Platform
platformPM.summary: Own the developer-facing roadmap end to end. SDKs, pricing tiers, console, billing — make every surface make sense to a half-asleep engineer at 2 a.m.

design.title: Senior Product Designer
design.summary: Visual system for the platform — docs, console, marketing, model cards. Tight loop with engineering, opinion on typography expected.

recruiter.title: Technical Recruiter · Engineering
recruiter.summary: We're hiring 18 over two quarters. Find them, brief them honestly, and protect their time. Senior-IC track if you want it.

office.heading: Gangnam, 35th floor.
office.body: {company}'s HQ sits in Nexora Tower, a fictional building on a real road — far enough from the convention center that lunch options exist, close enough to Gangnam station that the commute is one transfer. The 35th floor has the office; the 34th has the GPU pre-staging room nobody can show you.

floor.label: Headquarters floor
floor.footnote: The 34th is the GPU pre-staging room. Tours not offered.

coffee.label: Coffee machines
coffee.footnote: One Italian, one Korean, one experimental.

office.footnote: The building name is fictional; the road and postal code are not. Mail addressed to Nexora Tower may, in fact, arrive — which is more than we promised.

benefits.heading: What we offer.
benefits.body: Designed for a small team that ships — compensation and time-off that respect the unit economics, equipment and learning that respect your craft.

equity.name: Meaningful equity
equity.description: Stock options at every level, four-year vest with a one-year cliff. The dilution is real; so is the upside.

equipment.name: Apple equipment, your choice
equipment.description: MacBook Pro M4 Max plus the monitor of your choice — Studio Display, LG 5K UltraFine, or one of those ultrawide things if you must.

learning.name: $5,000 / year learning budget
learning.description: Conferences, books, courses, GPU credits for personal experiments. No approval required under $1,000.

wellness.name: Health + wellness, fully covered
wellness.description: Korean national health insurance plus private supplement. Mental health visits covered, dental and vision included, gym membership reimbursed.

childcare.name: Childcare support
childcare.description: Monthly stipend for under-5s, plus a 12-week parental leave at full pay. Used by people across the org, not stigmatized.

pto.name: Generous PTO + parental leave
pto.description: 25 days off + Korean public holidays + a one-week winter shutdown. Mostly unlimited beyond that, but we notice — we have a dashboard for it.

benefits.footnote: All benefits apply equally to full-time and 30+ hour contract employees after a 30-day probation.

hiringProcess.heading: Five steps. Three to four weeks, end to end.
hiringProcess.body: Same five steps regardless of role. Compensation is shared in step 1. We give feedback at every step, whether or not you advance.

application.name: Application
application.description: Send your resume or LinkedIn URL — no cover letter required. A real human reads every submission within one business day. Compensation range is shared in the response.

recruiterCall.name: Recruiter call
recruiterCall.description: Two-way fit. We explain the role, team, comp, and trade-offs honestly. You share what you're looking for. No technical questions — that's the next step.

technical.name: Technical conversation
technical.description: One or two engineers from the hiring team. Role-specific deep dive — code review, system design, research discussion, or product critique. No live coding gotchas; bring questions about real problems.

onsite.name: On-site
onsite.description: Four hours of conversation, one hour of awkward small talk, free lunch. Meet your team and your team's team. We make a decision within 48 hours of the on-site ending.

offer.name: Offer
offer.description: Verbal first, written within 24 hours. Negotiation expected — we publish bands internally, so we're working from the same numbers you are. Start date is flexible by up to 90 days.

hiringProcess.footnote: If you don't get an offer, we tell you why — concretely enough that you could try again in twelve months and know what changed.

finalCta.heading: Two paths in.
finalCta.body: Send your resume or refer someone you'd want to work with. Both inboxes are monitored daily by a real person.

apply.name: Apply to a role
apply.description: Pick a role above and send your resume directly to hiring. Or send a general application — we route it to the closest team.

refer.name: Refer someone
refer.description: Successful referrals get a $5,000 bonus, paid on the hire's 90-day mark. Referral track record is internally famous.

finalCta.footnote: We respond to every email within two business days. Korean and English both fine.
